provenance.source_corpus: Effects of harvesting and drought on CO<sub>2</sub> and H<sub>2</sub> O fluxes in an aspen-dominated western boreal plain forest: early chronosequence recovery

source_spans.text: The northern study area (NSA) and southern study area (SSA) were harvested in the winters of 2007 and 2008, respectively.

The results indicate that evapotranspiration fluxes and water-use efficiency rebounded quickly as a result of new vegetation foliage growth and wet conditions found within the first 2 years following the harvest.

cumulative annual precipitation also declined to 79% and 57% in 2009 and 2010, respectively, of the 30-year normal for that region, leading to significant (lagged) declines in forest productivity at the NSA in 2010 and 2011.
However, a period of dry years had a significant influence on rates of postharvest growth, carbon dioxide (CO2), and water fluxes at these sites.
Water-use efficiency also declined in 2010 and 2011
As long as soil moisture exceeds wilting-point values following harvest, ET and CO2 uptake increase with growth of foliage
As long as soil moisture exceeds wilting-point values following harvest, ET and CO2 uptake increase with growth of foliage
Growing-season cumulative GEP and NEP were positively linearly correlated with annual foliage growth (LAI e)
